commonMistakes.description: Raising the punching shoulder and tensing the trapezius throughout the strike, which slows arm extension and reduces power transfer from the core.
Failing to rotate the fist to a vertical-then-horizontal position at extension, leaving the wrist misaligned and reducing structural integrity on impact.
Leaning the torso backward to compensate for limited hip flexor mobility, reducing power and exposing the centerline.
Extending the leg fully before the hip reaches full flexion, causing a pushing action rather than a snapping strike.
Initiating the hooking arc from the knee rather than the hip, shortening the strike arc and losing the centrifugal momentum of the leg.
Pivoting the support foot insufficiently, which limits hip rotation and forces the body to compensate with lateral trunk lean.
Allowing the knees to cave inward (valgus collapse) rather than tracking over the toes, placing excessive stress on the medial knee ligaments.
Anteriorly tilting the pelvis and arching the lower back instead of maintaining a neutral spine with engaged abdominals.
Hyperextending the wrist backward at the moment of contact instead of maintaining a neutral wrist, which risks wrist sprain and diminishes force transmission.
Pushing the strike from the arm alone without engaging the pectorals and core rotation, producing a weak, arm-only strike with no whole-body power.
